Systems Touched: Salesforce (account metadata, contract dates), Zendesk (ticket history, CSAT scores), Mixpanel (product usage metrics, DAU/MAU, feature adoption), Slack (sentiment from customer channels)
Output/Deliverable: Health report with per-dimension scores (tickets/usage/sentiment) + executive summary + risk flags
Rationale: Strong across all four signals. Same steps every quarter for every account — only the data changes. Two CSMs using the same scoring rubric would produce near-identical assessments. All data sources have APIs. Report is reviewed internally before any client sees it.
Four Signals: Structural Repeatability: Strong — identical sequence for every account: pull CRM data, pull tickets, pull usage, pull Slack threads, score each dimension, write narrative, compile report
Four Signals: Determinism: Strong — output follows directly from data + scoring framework. Minimal subjective judgement in the scoring itself.
Four Signals: Data Availability: Strong — Salesforce API, Zendesk API, Mixpanel export API all available. Slack API for channel history.
Four Signals: Error Recoverability: Strong — report goes through internal review before reaching the client. Errors are catchable.
Sub-Workflow Split Candidate?: No — all steps are automatable with a single HIL review checkpoint before delivery
Notes: This is the workflow selected in the worked example. The consistent structure across accounts is what makes it automatable — not the fact that it is simple.

Trigger: Incoming alert — health score drop, support ticket escalation, or Slack flag from team member
Systems Touched: Salesforce (account history, relationship context, contract status), Zendesk (escalated ticket details, ticket history for the account), Slack (recent conversation context, who raised the flag)
Output/Deliverable: Triage assessment (severity rating + owner assignment + recommended action plan)
Rationale: Three of four signals are strong but determinism is weak. The triage flow is structurally repeatable (assess severity, check history, identify owner, propose action) but the actual judgement calls — how severe is this really, who should own it, what is the right response — depend on relationship history, political dynamics, and sometimes information that only exists in someone's head.
Four Signals: Structural Repeatability: Strong — same general flow every time: assess severity, check account history, identify owner, draft action plan
Four Signals: Determinism: Weak — two CSMs might make very different severity assessments and recommend different responses based on their knowledge of the account relationship and internal politics
Four Signals: Data Availability: Strong — ticket data, account history, and contract status all accessible via API
Four Signals: Error Recoverability: Strong — triage recommendation is reviewed by the CSM before any action is taken
Sub-Workflow Split Candidate?: No — judgement is interleaved throughout every step rather than concentrated at the end
Notes: An agent could prepare the initial data pack (pull ticket details, account history, recent interactions) but the CSM needs to be deeply involved at every decision point. This is a human-in-the-loop candidate, not a primarily automated workflow.

Trigger: Calendar — 90 days before contract renewal date
Systems Touched: Salesforce (contract details, ARR, renewal date, account tier), Zendesk (ticket trends over contract period), Mixpanel (usage trajectory, feature adoption trends), Finance system (billing history, payment status)
Output/Deliverable: Renewal brief (account health summary + usage trends + risk assessment + renewal recommendation) + risk register if applicable
Rationale: Strong across all four signals. Same preparation steps for every renewal. The brief follows a standard template. All key data has API access. The brief is reviewed by the account team before any renewal conversation.
Four Signals: Structural Repeatability: Strong — identical sequence regardless of account: pull contract details, compile usage trajectory, assess health trends, draft renewal brief
Four Signals: Determinism: Strong — the brief is data-driven and follows a template. Two CSMs would produce very similar briefs given the same data.
Four Signals: Data Availability: Strong — Salesforce, Zendesk, Mixpanel APIs cover all inputs. Finance system has a reporting API.
Four Signals: Error Recoverability: Strong — renewal brief is reviewed by account team and commercial lead before any client-facing conversation.
Sub-Workflow Split Candidate?: No — all steps are data gathering and structured analysis with a review checkpoint
Notes: Shares CRM, Zendesk, and Mixpanel data sources with Quarterly Health Review. Automating one builds reusable data-retrieval tools for the other.

Trigger: Quarterly review cycle or ad hoc trigger from product usage spike
Systems Touched: Mixpanel (usage patterns, feature adoption by tier, expansion signals), Salesforce (current contract scope, account tier, whitespace analysis), Product roadmap (upcoming features relevant to account)
Output/Deliverable: Opportunity brief (identified expansion areas + supporting evidence + recommended next steps)
Rationale: Structural repeatability is strong but determinism is weak. Identifying that usage of Feature X exceeds the current tier threshold is mechanical. But deciding whether that signal represents a genuine upsell opportunity — versus a temporary spike, a workaround for a missing feature, or usage that the customer does not value enough to pay more for — requires relationship context and commercial judgement.
Four Signals: Structural Repeatability: Strong — same data sources and analysis pattern each quarter: pull usage, identify signals above tier thresholds, cross-reference with contract scope, draft brief
Four Signals: Determinism: Weak — the same usage signal could be interpreted as a strong opportunity by one CSM and noise by another, depending on their knowledge of the account's priorities and budget cycle
Four Signals: Data Availability: Strong — Mixpanel and Salesforce APIs cover the quantitative inputs. Product roadmap is typically a spreadsheet or Notion page with less structured access.
Four Signals: Error Recoverability: Strong — opportunity brief is reviewed internally before any commercial conversation
Sub-Workflow Split Candidate?: Yes — data gathering and signal identification (High) could split from opportunity qualification and narrative (Low)
Notes: The data gathering portion (pull usage, flag signals above thresholds) is highly automatable. The qualification portion (is this a real opportunity?) requires the CSM's commercial judgement and relationship context.

Trigger: Weekly standup cadence — every Monday morning
Systems Touched: Jira (ticket status, sprint progress, blockers), Slack (recent updates from engineering and product channels), Internal docs (project tracker, decision log)
Output/Deliverable: Status summary (progress against milestones + active blockers + items needing discussion) + blockers list for escalation
Rationale: Strong across all four signals. Almost entirely data retrieval and reformatting. There is very little judgement involved — you are compiling facts, not interpreting them.
Four Signals: Structural Repeatability: Strong — identical steps every week: pull Jira status, scan Slack channels, check project tracker, compile summary
Four Signals: Determinism: Strong — two people pulling the same data would produce near-identical summaries. The output is factual, not interpretive.
Four Signals: Data Availability: Strong — Jira API, Slack API, project tracker is a shared spreadsheet or Notion page.
Four Signals: Error Recoverability: Strong — summary is shared in the standup meeting where errors are immediately visible and correctable.
Sub-Workflow Split Candidate?: No — all steps are data retrieval and reformatting
Notes: Despite being only 1 hour per occurrence, this scores High because of weekly frequency. High frequency + low complexity = strong automation candidate. 52 hours/year of near-mechanical work.

Systems Touched: Salesforce (account overview, contract timeline, key stakeholders), Zendesk (ticket trends, CSAT trajectory), Mixpanel (usage analytics, adoption trends), Finance (revenue data, billing history), Google Slides (presentation deck)
Output/Deliverable: EBR deck with data-backed narrative + talking points + appendix with supporting data
Rationale: High time cost but automation potential is only Medium. The data gathering portion (5-6 hours) is mechanical — same data sources, same metrics, same structure every time. But the narrative and deck creation portion (3-6 hours) requires deep relationship context, political sensitivity, and knowledge of what messaging will land with the specific executive sponsor.
Four Signals: Structural Repeatability: Mixed — data gathering is identical each time, but narrative structure varies based on what story needs to be told to this specific executive
Four Signals: Determinism: Weak overall — two CSMs would produce very different decks for the same exec because the narrative framing depends on relationship context and political dynamics
Four Signals: Data Availability: Strong for data, weak for narrative context — quantitative inputs have APIs, but the 'what story to tell' knowledge exists only in the CSM's head
Four Signals: Error Recoverability: Strong — deck is reviewed by account team and manager before the EBR
Sub-Workflow Split Candidate?: Yes — split into data gathering + initial analysis (High) and narrative + deck creation (Low)
Notes: Classic sub-workflow split candidate. The 5-6 hour data gathering portion becomes a strong automation target on its own. See the Sub-Workflow Pattern section in the methodology.

Trigger: Ad hoc — new stakeholder joining account, executive visit, or internal team transition
Systems Touched: Salesforce (account history, key contacts, contract details), Internal docs (previous meeting notes, decision log), Slack (recent account-related conversations)
Output/Deliverable: Briefing document (account overview + current status + key relationships + open issues + recent context)
Rationale: Strong across all four signals. The brief follows a standard template regardless of the trigger. The content is factual — account history, status, relationships — not interpretive. All data sources are accessible. The brief is reviewed before the meeting.
Four Signals: Structural Repeatability: Strong — same template every time: account overview, current status, key relationships, open issues, recent context
Four Signals: Determinism: Strong — the brief is factual compilation. Two CSMs would produce very similar briefings given the same data.
Four Signals: Data Availability: Strong — Salesforce, internal docs, and Slack all have API access or structured search.
Four Signals: Error Recoverability: Strong — briefing is reviewed before the meeting or handover.
Sub-Workflow Split Candidate?: No — all steps are data retrieval and structured compilation
Notes: Triggered ad hoc but the process is identical each time. Good automation candidate because it is time-sensitive (often needed quickly for an unexpected meeting) and the output is standardised.
